Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Tatjana Smith joins exclusive club of double SA gold medal winners

Tatjana Smith became South Africa’s most-successful Olympian of all time when she won gold in the 100m breaststroke at the Paris Olympics.

Tatjana Smithbecame South Africa’s most-successful Olympian of all time when she won gold in thewomen’s 100m breaststrokeat theParis Olympics 2024on Monday, 29 July.

Enjoy the Paris Olympic Games 2024 experience at your favourite ATKV Resort!

With two golds and a silver medal, she moves to the top of the all-time South African medal table.

As a reminder, at the Olympics, ranking is based ongold medals– with silver and bronze used as tie-breakers.

At Tokyo 2020, Smith won gold in the 200m breaststroke and took silver in the 100m breaststroke.

She currently holds the Olympic records in both events.

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section for free-to-read content

As reported byThe South African website, 27-year-old Tatjana Smith will contest her200m breaststrokeheats and semi-finals on Wednesday, 31 July with the final scheduled for Thursday, 1 August.

OnlyTHREEother South Africans have achieved double gold: Tennis playerCharles Winslowin 1912 (he added a bronze eight years later), swimmerPenny Heynsin 1996 (with a bronze in 2000), and 800m specialistCaster Semenya, who won in 2012 and 2016.

It’s worth noting thatChad le Closremains (for now) South Africa’s record holder in terms of total Olympic medals won, withfour.

Ahead of the Paris Olympics 2024, South Africa hadwon a total of 89 medals, comprised of 27 gold, 33 silver and 29 bronze.

Meanwhile, Smith pocketedR400 000for her win in the women’s 100m breaststroke thanks to the financial incentives promised by theSouth African Sports Confederation and Olympic Committee (Sascoc).

Smith’s coach, Rocco Meiring, will earn R100 000 for his role’s in Smith’s victory.

In addition, as reported byThe South African website, new Minister of Sport, Arts and Culture,Gayton McKenzie, promised that he has a ‘big surprise’ for all Team SA medal winners, suggesting there could be more prize money coming their way.

IndividualsGold medallists R400 000 and the coach R100 000Silver medallists R200 000 and the coach R50 000Bronze medallists R75 000 and the coach R25 000

title: Tatjana Smith joins exclusive club of double SA gold medal winners

description: Tatjana Smith became South Africa’s most-successful Olympian of all time when she won gold in the 100m breaststroke at the Paris Olympics.
